questions.question: Explain the structure of an inode and list at least five fields it contains.
keyPoints: Inode (index node) stores file metadata
Fields include: inode number, file type, permissions, timestamps (access, modify, change)
File size, link count, data block pointers, owner UID
Does not store filename - that is in directory entry
Each file has unique inode number in file system

questions.question: Describe three advantages and two disadvantages of indexed file allocation.
keyPoints: Advantages: Supports direct/random access, no external fragmentation, efficient for sequential access
Disadvantages: Wasted space for small files (index block overhead), extra disk I/O for index block access, single point failure (if index block corrupted)
Better than contiguous for large files requiring non-contiguous storage

questions.question: What is the purpose of the boot block and superblock in a file system? Differentiate between them.
keyPoints: Boot block: First sector containing bootstrap code for OS loading, present regardless of file system
Superblock: Contains file system metadata - total size, free blocks, free inodes, block size, mount status
Superblock is critical and duplicated for redundancy
Boot block is hardware-level, superblock is file system-level concept

questions.question: Compare contiguous and linked file allocation methods.
keyPoints: Contiguous: Files stored in consecutive blocks - fast random access but suffers external fragmentation
Linked: Files stored as linked list of blocks - no external fragmentation but no random access, pointer overhead
Contiguous allocation requires knowing file size beforehand
Linked allocation has poor random access performance due to sequential traversal

questions.question: Explain the hierarchical directory structure with a diagram example.
keyPoints: Hierarchical (tree) structure has root directory with nested subdirectories
Allows logical grouping of related files
Users can navigate using absolute or relative paths
Common in modern OS (Windows, Unix/Linux, Mac OS)
Supports file sharing through symbolic/hard links
Example: /home/user/documents/report.txt shows path from root

questions.question: Describe four techniques for free space management in file systems.
keyPoints: Bit Vector (Bitmap): One bit per block indicating free/allocated
Linked List: Each free block points to next free block
Grouping: First free block stores addresses of n other free blocks
Counting: Maintains (starting block, count) pairs for consecutive free blocks
Each method has trade-offs in speed and memory usage

questions.question: What is file system mounting? Explain the mounting process.
keyPoints: Mounting attaches a file system to the directory tree at a mount point
Operating system validates file system by reading superblock
Mount point is typically an empty directory
After mounting, files in that file system become accessible
Unmounting flushes buffers and updates superblock before detachment

questions.question: Explain the on-disk structure of a typical UNIX file system. Draw a diagram showing the organization and explain the purpose of each component.
keyPoints: Boot block: First sector for bootstrap code
Superblock: File system metadata (size, free inodes/blocks, block size)
Inode table: Array of inodes for all files
Data blocks: Actual file content storage
Boot block and superblock duplicated for reliability
Inodes contain metadata and direct/indirect block pointers
Directory structure implemented as special files containing (inode number, filename) pairs
Should include diagram showing: [Boot][Superblock][Inode Table][Data Blocks]

questions.question: Discuss in detail the three main file allocation methods: contiguous, linked, and indexed. Compare them in terms of external fragmentation, internal fragmentation, random access support, and access time complexity.
keyPoints: Contiguous: Consecutive blocks - O(1) random access, suffers external fragmentation
Linked: Blocks with pointers - no external fragmentation, no random access (O(n))
Indexed: Index block with pointers - supports random access (O(1)), internal fragmentation
External fragmentation: Contiguous worst, Linked/Indexed none
Internal fragmentation: Indexed worst (index block overhead), Contiguous minimal
Access time: Contiguous best for random, Linked best for sequential (no seek), Indexed moderate
All methods can be combined (e.g., linked with index blocks in UNIX)

questions.question: What is the Virtual File System (VFS)? Explain how it provides abstraction and allows multiple file system types to coexist. Also explain file sharing and protection mechanisms.
keyPoints: VFS is an abstraction layer providing uniform interface for different file systems
Applications interact with VFS without knowing underlying FS type (ext4, NTFS, FAT)
VFS defines standard operations (open, read, write, close) that each FS must implement
Supports file system mounting and integration
File sharing requires proper mechanisms: file locking (shared/exclusive), permission bits
Protection: User IDs, access control lists (ACLs), read/write/execute permissions
In distributed systems: needs concurrency control to prevent data inconsistency

questions.question: Explain the free space management techniques in detail with advantages and disadvantages of each.
keyPoints: Bit Vector: Fast allocation finding consecutive blocks but requires memory proportional to disk size
Linked List: Simple but cannot efficiently find contiguous blocks, traversing is slow
Grouping: Combines advantages - quick allocation of multiple blocks but complex implementation
Counting: Efficient for contiguous free space but poor for scattered blocks
Trade-off between memory usage and allocation speed
Modern file systems use combinations and journaling for reliability
Choice depends on typical file size and access patterns
